teamObjective.explanation: My hand contains only low non-trump spades (3-6) with no high cards, trumps, or bombs. On the opening lead, playing the lowest single card gives my partner the best chance to win the trick with a modest card while opponents may duck or be unable to follow.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner likely holds stronger cards than my very weak hand and can potentially take control of the trick.
evidence: My hand has only ranks 3-6 of spades, all non-trump.
Opening hand counts show partner has 3 cards, same as opponents.
No public cards have been played, so partner strength is unknown but my hand is unusually weak, suggesting partner may have more of the team's high cards.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents likely hold higher cards and trumps; they can probably beat any card I lead, but may choose not to on a very low lead.
evidence: I hold no trumps and only low ranks 3-6.
Each opponent has 3 cards in a fresh deck.
Leading the 3 leaves maximal room for opponents to potentially duck rather than commit a high card.

actionRationale.primaryReason: The 3 of spades is the lowest card in my hand. Leading it minimizes the risk of an opponent winning with a marginally higher card and preserves my slightly higher cards (4-6) for later tricks. It also gives partner the widest window to play a winning card if opponents do not take the trick.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: The 4 is still low and would be beaten by the same higher cards, while using up a card that could win if an opponent leads a 3 later.
Leading the 5 wastes a card that might be useful later and does not improve the chance of winning this trick.
The 6 is my highest card; using it now on a weak lead would leave me even more defenseless on future tricks and is unlikely to win against opponents.

risks: An opponent may play a higher single or trump to win the lead immediately.
Partner may not be able to win if opponents commit a card higher than anything partner holds.
Leading low may allow the opponent who plays second to gain cheap control.
riskAssessment.mitigation: By choosing the absolute lowest card, I force opponents to spend a higher card if they want the lead, and I keep my 4-6 for future plays. If partner can win, the low lead maximizes that opportunity.

notes: First decision of the game with no public information; conservative low lead is standard with a very weak hand.
